ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for spicy braised pork intestine casserole; closest defensible match is pork intestine food product (FOODON_03315595).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers pig intestine as a raw commodity; no prepared braised casserole variant exists in FoodEx2. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for spicy braised pork intestine casserole dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of spicy braised pork intestine casserole is approximately 200 g, representing a standard individual portion as served in a Chinese restaurant or hawker setting.

nutrient_source.title: 香辣肥肠煲 Spicy Braised Pork Intestine Casserole — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (cr_id G00010150)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 香辣肥肠煲 / Spicy Braised Pork Intestine Casserole (cr_id G00010150, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical). All per-100g values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are taken as reported by HPB. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100g) is not reported by HPB for this entry and has been estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine (FDC ID 168374) as a supplementary approved source; this estimation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010150)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Spicy Braised Pork Intestine Casserole contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100g as reported by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010150). Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content per standard GI methodology (University of Sydney GI Database protocol requires minimum 50 g available carbohydrate for GI testing). Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI value is assigned; glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; doubanjiang and soy sauce are both intrinsically high-sodium ingredients
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this dish or consume only very small portions; the sodium content of 1050 mg/100g means a standard 200 g serving delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and the spicy chili components may additionally cause acute blood pressure elevation in sensitive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar per 100g with high protein (19.1 g/100g) result in negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has negligible glycaemic impact due to 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100g; however, patients with Type 2 Diabetes should practice portion control given the moderate fat content (10.5 g/100g) and very high sodium (1050 mg/100g), and should confirm no starch thickeners are added to the braising sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), cholesterol (89 mg/100g), and saturated fat (~3.5 g/100g) from pork offal; pork intestine is a high-cholesterol organ meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the combination of very high sodium (1050 mg/100g), significant dietary cholesterol (89 mg/100g), and saturated fat from pork large intestine, all of which contribute to elevated cardiovascular risk; the low potassium content (47 mg/100g) provides no meaningful cardioprotective offset.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork offal high in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); chili and doubanjiang are nightshade-derived and may trigger inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as pork intestine is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory pathways, and the doubanjiang and dried chili ingredients are nightshade-derived and may exacerbate joint inflammation and swelling, compounded by the high sodium content (1050 mg/100g).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Braised Pork Intestine Casserole (香辣肥肠煲, Xiāng là féi cháng bāo) is a bold, intensely flavoured Chinese dish featuring cleaned and pre-cooked pork large intestine slow-braised in a spicy, aromatic sauce. The sauce is built on doubanjiang (fermented chili bean paste), dried chilies, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and Sichuan peppercorn, producing a deep red, numbing-spicy (málà) flavour profile characteristic of Sichuan-influenced Chinese cooking. The intestine develops a tender, glutinous, slightly chewy texture through prolonged braising, absorbing the complex umami and chili notes of the sauce. The dish is typically served in a claypot or casserole vessel to retain heat, and is a popular lunch and dinner option in Chinese restaurants and hawker centres in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein (19.1 g/100g), moderate-fat (10.5 g/100g) dish with zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it glycaemically inert. However, the sodium content is very high at 1050 mg/100g, driven by the doubanjiang and soy sauce components, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The cholesterol content of 89 mg/100g reflects the organ-meat nature of pork intestine. This dish is not halal and contains soy allergens from the doubanjiang and soy sauce. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010150, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine (FDC ID 168374) as a supplementary approved source, documented in notes.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010150); pork intestine contains no carbohydrate by composition and the braising sauce components (doubanjiang, soy sauce, chili) do not contribute measurable carbohydrate at this preparation level — confirmed zero per HPB. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per the same HPB source. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100g confirmed by HPB); GI testing requires a minimum of 50 g available carbohydrate per University of Sydney GI Database protocol. Saturated_fat_g (3.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine (FDC ID 168374) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry; this is an approximation and should be verified against direct laboratory analysis. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this prepared dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are functional translations provided for multilingual coverage; native-speaker verification is recommended. This dish is not halal due to pork content.
